claims.text: Morally appropriate behavior will not harm others, but instead increase happiness or utility.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach

claims.text: Promoting human happiness was incumbent on us since it was approved by God.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Theological Utilitarians

claims.text: A full and complete obligation which will extend to all cases can only arise from the authority of God, because God only can in all cases make a man happy or miserable.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - John Gay

claims.text: The happiness of mankind is the criterion of virtue, but once removed (through God's will).
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - John Gay

claims.text: A person's individual salvation and eternal happiness depended on conformity to God's will, as did virtue itself.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - John Gay

claims.text: Divine beneficence consists in God's promotion of happiness.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Susanna Newcome

claims.text: The moral duties we have are duties to promote happiness.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Susanna Newcome

claims.text: Our duties to help those in need are quite stringent.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Susanna Newcome

claims.text: There is a moral imperative to consider the suffering of animals when engaged in practical deliberation.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Susanna Newcome

claims.text: It is not clear what essential work God does in the theological approach to utilitarianism, at least in terms of normative ethics.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Theological Approach Critique

claims.text: God as the source of normativity is compatible with utilitarianism, but utilitarianism does not require this.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Theological Approach Critique

claims.text: Some expressions of virtue and vice are to be either praised or condemned regardless of consequences.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Bishop Joseph Butler

claims.text: There are certain dispositions of mind and certain actions which are in themselves approved or disapproved by mankind, abstracted from the consideration of their tendency to the happiness or misery of the world.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Bishop Joseph Butler

claims.text: If production of happiness was all that mattered for moral approval, then acts of treachery would be approved of as long as they produced more good than not, which is clearly not the case.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Bishop Joseph Butler

claims.text: Promise keeping is morally good independently of its consequences.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Richard Price

claims.text: There are some actions that are impermissible by their nature, intrinsically.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Richard Price

claims.text: If happiness is really the only reason for our being on this earth, we should take very seriously the idea that other things we think important are so only because of their relation to happiness.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Roger Crisp on Price

claims.text: We possess a kind of 'inner eye' that allows us to make moral discriminations.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Shaftesbury

claims.text: If an act is vicious, its viciousness is a matter of the human response (given a corrected perspective) to the act or its perceived effects, and thus has a kind of contingency.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Hume

claims.text: The virtuous person contributes to the good of the whole.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Shaftesbury

claims.text: It is the private interest and good of everyone to work towards the general good.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Shaftesbury

claims.text: If a creature ceases to promote the general good, he is actually so far wanting to himself, and ceases to promote his own happiness and welfare.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Shaftesbury

claims.text: To be virtuous a person must have certain psychological capacities including the ability to reflect on character and represent to themselves the qualities in others that are either approved or disapproved of.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Shaftesbury

claims.text: Animals are not objects of moral appraisal since they lack the necessary reflective capacities.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Shaftesbury

claims.text: The virtuous person is one whose affections, motives, and dispositions are of the right sort, not one whose behavior is simply of the right sort.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Shaftesbury

claims.text: A person who harms others through no fault of his own (such as through convulsive fits) is not vicious since he has no desire to harm anyone and his bodily movements are beyond his control.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Shaftesbury

claims.text: In equal degrees of happiness expected to proceed from an action, the virtue is in proportion to the number of persons to whom the happiness shall extend.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Francis Hutcheson

claims.text: In equal numbers, the virtue is in proportion to the quantity of the happiness or natural good.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Francis Hutcheson

claims.text: That action is best which procures the greatest happiness for the greatest numbers, and that worst which occasions the most misery.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Francis Hutcheson

claims.text: The moral sense causes us to look with favor on actions which benefit others and disfavor those which harm them, while consequentialist reasoning determines a more precise ranking order of practical options in given situations.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Francis Hutcheson

claims.text: The moral sense is concerned with motives, and we approve of the motive of benevolence, with wider scope being better.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Francis Hutcheson

claims.text: The dignity or moral importance of persons may compensate numbers in moral calculation.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Francis Hutcheson

claims.text: We have a duty to others in virtue of their personhood to accord them fundamental dignity regardless of the numbers of others whose happiness is to be affected by the action in question.
claims.section_context: Precursors to the Classical Approach - Francis Hutcheson

claims.text: Humans are ruled by two sovereign masters: pleasure and pain.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - Jeremy Bentham

claims.text: Pleasure and pain govern us in all we do, in all we say, in all we think.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - Jeremy Bentham

claims.text: Actions are approved when they are such as to promote happiness or pleasure, and disapproved of when they have a tendency to cause unhappiness or pain.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - Jeremy Bentham

claims.text: We should be actively trying to promote overall happiness.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - Jeremy Bentham

claims.text: Psychological egoism rules out acting to promote overall well-being when that is incompatible with one's own interests.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - Jeremy Bentham

claims.text: People do sometimes act benevolently, with the overall good of humanity in mind.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - Jeremy Bentham

claims.text: A person's true character is known, if known at all, only by that person.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - Jeremy Bentham

claims.text: Character, while theoretically very interesting, is not a practical focus for legislation.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - Jeremy Bentham

claims.text: Focus on character would be socially disruptive if one viewed someone who disagreed on a moral issue as defective in character rather than simply making a mistake reflected in action.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - Jeremy Bentham

claims.text: The one intrinsic good is pleasure; the bad is pain.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - Jeremy Bentham

claims.text: We are to promote pleasure and act to reduce pain.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - Jeremy Bentham

claims.text: When making a moral decision one measures an action's value with respect to pleasure and pain according to intensity, duration, certainty, proximity, fecundity, purity, and extent.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - Jeremy Bentham

claims.text: It is not the case that there is a particular kind of action that is intrinsically wrong; actions that are wrong are wrong simply in virtue of their effects.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - Jeremy Bentham

claims.text: Liberty and autonomy are good instrumentally, not intrinsically.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - Jeremy Bentham

claims.text: Any action deemed wrong due to a violation of autonomy is derivatively wrong on instrumental grounds.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - Jeremy Bentham

claims.text: Since effects of a given policy may change, the moral quality of the policy may change as well.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - Jeremy Bentham

claims.text: A law that is good at one point in time may be a bad law at some other point in time.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - Jeremy Bentham

claims.text: Lawmakers have to be sensitive to changing social circumstances.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - Jeremy Bentham

claims.text: There are no qualitative differences between pleasures, only quantitative ones.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - John Stuart Mill - Criticism of Bentham

claims.text: There are some pleasures that are more fitting than others.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - John Stuart Mill

claims.text: Intellectual pleasures are of a higher, better sort than the ones that are merely sensual and that we share with animals.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - John Stuart Mill

claims.text: Those persons who have experienced both intellectual and sensual pleasures view the higher as better than the lower.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - John Stuart Mill

claims.text: It is better to be Socrates dissatisfied than a fool satisfied.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - John Stuart Mill

claims.text: The only proof capable of being given that anything is desirable is that people do actually desire it.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - John Stuart Mill

claims.text: People desire happiness.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - John Stuart Mill

claims.text: The general happiness is a good to the aggregate of all persons.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - John Stuart Mill

claims.text: 'Desirable' does not mean 'able to be desired' as 'visible' means 'able to be seen'; the desirable means simply what ought to be desired or deserves to be desired.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - G.E. Moore's Criticism of Mill

claims.text: Internal sanctions like guilt and remorse serve to regulate our actions and are instrumentally very important to appropriate action.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - John Stuart Mill

claims.text: We are the sorts of beings that have social feelings and feelings for others, not just ourselves.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - John Stuart Mill

claims.text: Natural features of human psychology, such as conscience and a sense of justice, underwrite motivation.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - John Stuart Mill

claims.text: The sense of justice results from very natural impulses including a desire to punish those who have harmed others.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - John Stuart Mill

claims.text: We can be said to have certain rights, but those rights are underwritten by utility.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - John Stuart Mill

claims.text: If one can show that a purported right or duty is harmful, then one has shown that it is not genuine.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - John Stuart Mill

claims.text: The ideal marriage exists between individuals of cultivated faculties who influence each other equally.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - John Stuart Mill

claims.text: Denying women access to education and other opportunities for development is forgoing a significant source of happiness.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - John Stuart Mill

claims.text: The correct appeal in moral justification is to utility itself, not to natural order or social traditions.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - John Stuart Mill

claims.text: Virtue not only has instrumental value but is constitutive of the good life.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - John Stuart Mill

claims.text: Virtue is to be desired disinterestedly, for itself.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - John Stuart Mill

claims.text: A person without virtue is morally lacking and is not as able to promote the good.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - John Stuart Mill

claims.text: Actions are right in proportion as they tend to promote happiness, wrong as they tend to produce the reverse of happiness.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - John Stuart Mill

claims.text: The right action is the action to be performed that promotes happiness overall, the greatest happiness for the greatest number of people.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - John Stuart Mill

claims.text: What is just or right is a function of what produces the greatest quantity of good.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - William Godwin

claims.text: Right is to be understood in terms of production of the good, specifically maximization of the good.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - William Godwin

claims.text: We ought show no preference for one person over another simply in virtue of our personal interest, everything being equal.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - William Godwin

claims.text: This commitment to impartiality allows for all kinds of preference for one person over another when we take into consideration the wider impact of our actions.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - William Godwin

claims.text: We ought to save the Archbishop over the chambermaid because that will have more of a positive impact on society.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - William Godwin

claims.text: As rational beings, we are committed to acting justly in a way that employs the exercise of our own understanding.
claims.section_context: The Classical Approach - William Godwin

claims.text: A simple reliance on intuition cannot resolve fundamental conflicts between values or rules such as Truth and Justice that may conflict.
claims.section_context: Henry Sidgwick

claims.text: We require some higher principle to decide the issue when values or rules conflict, and that will be utilitarianism.
claims.section_context: Henry Sidgwick

claims.text: The rules which seem to be a fundamental part of common sense morality are often vague and underdescribed, and applying them will actually require appeal to something theoretically more basic: utilitarianism.
claims.section_context: Henry Sidgwick

claims.text: Absolute interpretations of rules seem highly counter-intuitive, and we need some justification for any exceptions, provided by utilitarianism.
claims.section_context: Henry Sidgwick

claims.text: The opinion that secrecy may render an action right which would not otherwise be so should itself be kept comparatively secret.
claims.section_context: Henry Sidgwick

claims.text: It is expedient that the doctrine that esoteric morality is expedient should itself be kept esoteric.
claims.section_context: Henry Sidgwick

claims.text: A utilitarian may reasonably desire, on utilitarian principles, that some of his conclusions should be rejected by mankind generally.
claims.section_context: Henry Sidgwick

claims.text: It may be best if people do not believe utilitarianism, even though it is true.
claims.section_context: Henry Sidgwick

claims.text: We should follow the course that we predict will have the best outcome, given as part of our calculations the data that others may fail in some way.
claims.section_context: Henry Sidgwick

claims.text: Assuming that the average happiness of human beings is a positive quantity, if the average happiness remains undiminished, utilitarianism directs us to make the number enjoying it as great as possible.
claims.section_context: Henry Sidgwick

claims.text: If the additional population enjoys on the whole positive happiness, we ought to weigh the amount of happiness gained by the extra number against the amount lost by the remainder.
claims.section_context: Henry Sidgwick

claims.text: We should increase population to the point where we maximize the product of the number of persons who are currently alive and the amount of average happiness.
claims.section_context: Henry Sidgwick

claims.text: We ought to promote the good, but the good includes far more than what could be reduced to pleasure.
claims.section_context: Ideal Utilitarianism - G.E. Moore

claims.text: Beauty is an intrinsic good.
claims.section_context: Ideal Utilitarianism - G.E. Moore

claims.text: A beautiful object has value independent of any pleasure it might generate in a viewer.
claims.section_context: Ideal Utilitarianism - G.E. Moore

claims.text: A beautiful world is better than an ugly world, even though no one is around to appreciate its beauty.
claims.section_context: Ideal Utilitarianism - G.E. Moore

claims.text: An empty universe is better than a universe of sadists, even though there is a good deal of pleasure and no pain in the universe of sadists.
claims.section_context: Ideal Utilitarianism - G.E. Moore

claims.text: What is necessary for the good is at least the absence of bad intentionality.
claims.section_context: Ideal Utilitarianism - G.E. Moore

claims.text: The pleasures of sadists, in virtue of their desires to harm others, get discounted and are not good, even though they are pleasures.
claims.section_context: Ideal Utilitarianism - G.E. Moore

claims.text: A whole has an intrinsic value different in amount from the sum of the values of its parts.
claims.section_context: Ideal Utilitarianism - G.E. Moore

claims.text: One cannot determine the value of a body by adding up the value of its parts.
claims.section_context: Ideal Utilitarianism - G.E. Moore

claims.text: The experience of beauty is better when the object of the experience, the beautiful object, actually exists.
claims.section_context: Ideal Utilitarianism - G.E. Moore

claims.text: A true belief in the reality of an object greatly increases the value of many valuable wholes.
claims.section_context: Ideal Utilitarianism - G.E. Moore

claims.text: Deluded happiness would be severely lacking in comparison to happiness based on knowledge.
claims.section_context: Ideal Utilitarianism - G.E. Moore

claims.text: The articulation and systematic development of the secular, forward-looking approach to policy formation is owed to the Classical Utilitarians.
claims.section_context: Conclusion

relations.note: Divine approval provides theological justification for the principle that morally appropriate behavior increases happiness.
God's unique ability to affect happiness in all cases explains why promoting happiness requires divine authority.
Specifies that happiness is the criterion but mediated through God's will rather than directly.
Individual salvation depending on God's will explains why virtue relates to happiness through divine mediation.
If God's beneficence consists in promoting happiness, then our moral duties mirror this divine attribute.
The duty to promote happiness is a specific instance of the general principle that morally appropriate behavior increases happiness.
Stringent duties to help those in need exemplify the broader duty to promote happiness.
Considering animal suffering is a specific application of the duty to promote happiness.
Directly questions whether God plays essential normative role in theological utilitarianism.
Addresses the objection by clarifying God's role is optional for utilitarian theory.
Butler challenges happiness-based morality by asserting intrinsic virtue/vice independent of consequences.
Provides the theoretical basis for Butler's objection about intrinsic approval/disapproval.
Treachery example concretely illustrates Butler's point about actions having intrinsic moral status.
Price's claim that promise-keeping has consequence-independent value contradicts utility-based morality.
The general principle of intrinsic impermissibility supports Price's specific claim about promise-keeping.
Crisp's response challenges Price by suggesting we should reconsider whether other values are merely instrumental to happiness.
Contributing to the good of the whole aligns with not harming others and increasing utility.
Self-interest aligning with general good explains why virtuous persons contribute to the whole.
Failing to promote general good harming one's own welfare explains why self-interest aligns with it.
The claim about animals requires the definition of virtue as requiring reflective capacities.
The convulsive fits case exemplifies the principle that virtue concerns affections not mere behavior.
The principle about virtue proportional to number of persons supports the greatest happiness formula.
The principle about virtue proportional to quantity of happiness supports the greatest happiness formula.
Hutcheson's greatest happiness principle specifies how to increase happiness for the greatest numbers.
Explains the psychological mechanism (moral sense) through which the greatest happiness principle operates.
Dignity compensating for numbers qualifies the simple maximization implied by greatest happiness.
Fundamental dignity duties explain why dignity may compensate numbers in moral calculation.
If pleasure and pain rule us, then approving actions that promote pleasure follows naturally.
The comprehensive governance of pleasure/pain in all domains supports their sovereignty.
Bentham's principle specifies happiness in terms of pleasure and pain.
Active promotion strengthens the passive approval/disapproval stated in claim 36.
Psychological egoism contradicts the demand to promote overall well-being when it conflicts with self-interest.
Evidence of benevolent action contradicts psychological egoism.
Character not being practical for legislation depends on character being epistemically inaccessible.
Identifying pleasure as the intrinsic good grounds the principle that actions promoting pleasure are approved.
Adds the active duty to reduce pain alongside promoting pleasure.
The hedonic calculus provides methodology for implementing the duty to promote pleasure.
No intrinsically wrong actions depends on pleasure being the only intrinsic good.
Liberty being only instrumentally good supports that no actions are intrinsically wrong.
Autonomy violations being derivatively wrong requires autonomy being only instrumentally valuable.
Policies' moral quality changing with effects supports that actions are wrong only due to effects.
Laws' moral quality changing over time exemplifies the general principle about policy effects.
Need for sensitivity to circumstances depends on laws' moral status being time-relative.
Mill challenges Bentham by denying only quantitative differences exist between pleasures.
Mill's claim about qualitative differences directly opposes Bentham's quantitative-only view.
Intellectual pleasures being higher exemplifies the general claim about qualitative differences.
Experienced persons preferring higher pleasures provides empirical support for their superiority.
The Socrates example vividly illustrates intellectual pleasures being superior to sensual ones.
The methodological claim about proof supports establishing that people desire happiness.
People desiring happiness supports that general happiness is good for the aggregate.
Moore objects that Mill commits naturalistic fallacy by treating 'desirable' like 'visible'.
Social feelings support our ability to promote overall happiness rather than just our own.
Natural psychological features like conscience explain the operation of internal sanctions.
The desire to punish harm exemplifies natural impulses underlying the sense of justice.
Showing harm defeats a right only if rights are underwritten by utility.
Denying women opportunities forgoing happiness supports the ideal of equal marriage.
Appealing to utility itself rather than tradition supports that utility underwrites rights.
Virtue being constitutive of good life challenges pleasure as sole intrinsic good.
Virtue being desired for itself supports its constitutive rather than merely instrumental value.
Virtue being necessary for promoting good supports that it should be desired for itself.
Mill's principle of utility explicitly formulates the relationship between right action and happiness.
Greatest happiness for greatest number specifies how to apply the proportionality principle.
Justice being a function of maximizing good supports the greatest happiness principle.
Explicates that right understood as maximization is the core of Godwin's position.
Impartiality principle supports maximization by preventing personal preference from distorting calculations.
Qualifies impartiality by noting it allows preferences when considering wider impact.
Archbishop case exemplifies how wider impact justifies preferences under impartiality.
Sidgwick challenges intuition-based morality that Butler relies on for intrinsic virtue claims.
Utilitarianism as higher principle directly addresses the problem of conflicting intuitions.
Vagueness of common sense rules requiring utilitarian resolution supports utilitarianism as higher principle.
Need to justify exceptions to absolute rules supports utilitarianism providing theoretical foundation.
Esoteric morality being kept secret exemplifies the general point about concealing utilitarian truth.
Makes explicit the recursive nature of keeping esoteric morality secret.
Reasonable desire for rejection of conclusions supports the general claim about concealment.
Specifies how greatest happiness principle applies to population size questions.
Adds nuance about weighing happiness lost against happiness gained in population cases.
Provides specific formula for maximizing the product of population and average happiness.
Moore's ideal utilitarianism directly challenges hedonistic reduction of good to pleasure.
Beauty as intrinsic good exemplifies Moore's expansion beyond pleasure.
Beauty having value independent of pleasure supports it being intrinsically good.
Beautiful world without viewers vividly illustrates beauty's pleasure-independent value.
Empty universe being better than pleasurable sadist universe contradicts pleasure as sole good.
Need for absence of bad intentionality explains why sadist universe is not good despite pleasure.
Discounting sadistic pleasures explains the judgment in the thought experiment.
Organic unity principle directly supports why parts don't sum to whole value.
Beauty experience with real object exemplifies organic unity where whole exceeds parts.
True belief increasing value explains why real beautiful objects enhance experience value.
Deluded happiness being inferior exemplifies how true belief enhances valuable wholes.

arguments.name: Theological Utilitarianism
arguments.summary: Moral obligations to promote happiness derive their normative force from God's authority and will. Since God alone can affect happiness in all cases and divine beneficence consists in promoting happiness, our moral duties are to promote happiness as commanded by God.

arguments.name: Butler's Intrinsic Virtue Objection
arguments.summary: Some expressions of virtue and vice have intrinsic moral status independent of their consequences. Certain dispositions and actions are approved or disapproved by their nature, not merely by their tendency to produce happiness or misery, as shown by cases like treachery.

arguments.name: Price's Deontological Objection
arguments.summary: Some actions, such as promise-keeping, are morally good independently of their consequences. There are actions that are intrinsically permissible or impermissible by their very nature, not merely because of their effects on happiness.

arguments.name: Shaftesbury's Virtue and Public Good Argument
arguments.summary: The virtuous person contributes to the good of the whole, and doing so is actually in each person's private interest. Virtue consists in having the right affections and motives oriented toward the public good, not merely performing right actions.

arguments.name: Hutcheson's Greatest Happiness Principle
arguments.summary: The best action is that which procures the greatest happiness for the greatest numbers. Virtue is proportional to both the number of persons affected and the quantity of happiness produced, though dignity may compensate for numbers in moral calculation.

arguments.name: Bentham's Hedonic Utilitarianism
arguments.summary: Humans are governed by pleasure and pain in all they think, say, and do. The one intrinsic good is pleasure and the bad is pain. Actions are right insofar as they promote pleasure and wrong insofar as they produce pain, with no actions being intrinsically wrong apart from their effects.

arguments.name: Bentham's Consequentialist Legal Theory
arguments.summary: The moral quality of laws and policies depends entirely on their effects, which can change over time. A law that is good at one point may become bad at another as social circumstances change, requiring lawmakers to be sensitive to changing conditions.

arguments.name: Mill's Qualitative Hedonism
arguments.summary: Contrary to Bentham, there are qualitative differences between pleasures, not merely quantitative ones. Intellectual pleasures are of a higher, better sort than sensual pleasures, as confirmed by those who have experienced both types preferring the higher pleasures.

arguments.name: Mill's Proof of Utilitarianism
arguments.summary: The only proof that something is desirable is that people actually desire it. Since people desire happiness, and each person's happiness is good to that person, the general happiness is good to the aggregate of all persons.

arguments.name: Mill's Naturalistic Motivation Theory
arguments.summary: Natural features of human psychology, including social feelings, conscience, and a sense of justice rooted in desires to punish harm, provide the motivational foundation for utilitarian action. Internal sanctions like guilt instrumentally regulate our behavior toward promoting overall good.

arguments.name: Mill's Utility-Based Rights Theory
arguments.summary: We can be said to have rights, but those rights are underwritten by utility rather than being independent moral facts. If one can show that a purported right or duty is harmful, then one has shown it is not genuine. The correct appeal in moral justification is to utility itself.

arguments.name: Mill's Virtue as Constitutive Good
arguments.summary: Virtue has not only instrumental value but is constitutive of the good life itself. Virtue is to be desired disinterestedly for itself, and a person without virtue is morally lacking and less able to promote the good.

arguments.name: Mill's Principle of Utility
arguments.summary: Actions are right in proportion as they tend to promote happiness, wrong as they tend to produce the reverse. The right action is that which promotes the greatest happiness for the greatest number of people.

arguments.name: Godwin's Impartial Maximization
arguments.summary: What is just or right is determined by what produces the greatest quantity of good through maximization. We should show no preference for one person over another based on personal interest, though impartiality allows preferences when considering wider social impact.

arguments.name: Sidgwick's Utilitarian Intuitionism
arguments.summary: Simple reliance on moral intuition cannot resolve fundamental conflicts between values or rules. Common sense moral rules are often vague and require appeal to a higher principle for application and exception-making. Utilitarianism provides this theoretical foundation as the higher principle that resolves moral conflicts.

arguments.name: Sidgwick's Esoteric Morality
arguments.summary: It may be best, on utilitarian grounds, if people do not believe utilitarianism even though it is true. The opinion that secrecy may render an action right should itself be kept secret, and a utilitarian may reasonably desire that some utilitarian conclusions be rejected by mankind generally.

arguments.name: Sidgwick's Total Utilitarianism
arguments.summary: Assuming average happiness is positive and remains undiminished, utilitarianism directs us to make the number enjoying it as great as possible. We should increase population to maximize the product of the number of persons alive and the amount of average happiness, weighing happiness gained by additional people against happiness lost by existing people.

arguments.name: Moore's Ideal Utilitarianism
arguments.summary: We ought to promote the good, but the good includes far more than pleasure and cannot be reduced to it. Beauty is an intrinsic good with value independent of any pleasure it generates. A beautiful world is better than an ugly one even without observers, and an empty universe is better than one of sadists despite their pleasure.

arguments.name: Moore's Organic Unity Principle
arguments.summary: A whole has an intrinsic value different in amount from the sum of the values of its parts. One cannot determine the value of a complex state by simply adding up the value of its components. For example, the experience of beauty is more valuable when the beautiful object actually exists, and true belief greatly increases the value of many valuable wholes.
